questions.question: Explain the need for balanced search trees. Why don't we use simple Binary Search Trees for all applications?
keyPoints: BST can become unbalanced with O(n) worst-case operations
Ordered data leads to linear tree (skewed)
Balanced trees guarantee O(log n) for all operations
Example: inserting sorted 1,2,3,4,5 creates skewed tree

questions.question: Define AVL tree and explain the balance factor condition.
keyPoints: AVL tree is a self-balancing BST
Balance factor = height(left subtree) - height(right subtree)
For every node, balance factor must be -1, 0, or +1
If any node violates this, rebalancing is required

questions.question: List and explain the four rotation cases in AVL trees with appropriate examples.
keyPoints: LL Case: Left-left imbalance - single right rotation
RR Case: Right-right imbalance - single left rotation
LR Case: Left-right imbalance - double rotation (left then right)
RL Case: Right-left imbalance - double rotation (right then left)

questions.question: What are the five properties of Red-Black trees? Explain any two in detail.
keyPoints: 1) Every node is either red or black
2) Root is black
3) All leaf (NIL) nodes are black
4) Red node cannot have red children
5) Every path from node to leaves has equal black-height
Any two properties explained with reasoning

questions.question: Explain the B-Tree properties. What is the minimum degree (t) and how does it affect the structure?
keyPoints: All leaves at same level (balanced)
Each node has at most 2t children
Each node (except root) has at least t-1 keys
Root has at least 1 key if not leaf
Keys within node are sorted

questions.question: Compare AVL trees and Red-Black trees with respect to balance strictness and operations.
keyPoints: AVL more strictly balanced (height diff ≤1)
Red-Black less strict but still guarantees O(log n)
AVL faster lookups but more rotations during insert
Red-Black fewer rotations, better for frequent insertions

questions.question: Explain the split operation in B-Trees with minimum degree t=3.
keyPoints: Split occurs when node has 2t-1 = 5 keys (full)
Split divides node into two nodes with t-1 = 2 keys each
Middle key moves up to parent
This maintains balance property

questions.question: Construct an AVL tree by inserting the following keys in order: 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 25. Show all rotations performed.
keyPoints: Insert 10 - no rotation
Insert 20 - no rotation (balanced)
Insert 30 - RR case, left rotation needed
Insert 40 - no rotation
Insert 50 - RR case, left rotation needed
Insert 25 - LR case, double rotation needed
Final balanced tree structure

questions.question: Explain the insertion operation in Red-Black trees. Discuss the cases and how violations are resolved.
keyPoints: Insert new node as red (except root which is black)
Check for violations: red-red or root property
Case 1: Parent and uncle both red - recolor
Case 2: Uncle black, triangle (LR/LL) - single rotation + recolor
Case 3: Uncle black, line (RR/LL) - double rotation + recolor
Continue up the tree if needed

questions.question: Explain B-Tree insertion operation. How do you handle overflow when inserting a key?
keyPoints: Start from root, search for appropriate leaf
Insert key in sorted order within node
If leaf overflows (more than 2t-1 keys), split
Split: divide into two nodes, move middle key to parent
If parent overflows, propagate split upward
All leaves remain at same level

questions.question: Insert the following keys into an empty B-Tree with minimum degree t=3: 10, 20, 5, 6, 12, 30, 7, 17. Show the final tree structure.
keyPoints: t=3 means max keys=5, min keys=2 per node
Insert 10,20 - fits in root
Insert 5 - overflow, split root
Insert 6,12 - add to appropriate nodes
Insert 30 - causes split
Insert 7,17 - continue adding and splitting as needed
Final tree with all leaves at same level
